segments.text: Design deliberation for session sigma-1-design-1775439447. 3 design decision points were reviewed.

segments.speaker: designer
segments.label: Designer
segments.text:
# Sigma-1 Design Recommendations

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-12
category: ux-behavior
question: What user interaction pattern should the website use for the self-service quote builder?
recommendation: A

OPTION_A:
label: "Multi-step wizard with persistent summary sidebar"
description: A 3-step progressive flow — (1) Select equipment from catalog with search/filter, (2) Choose dates and delivery details, (3) Review and submit. A persistent sidebar on desktop (bottom sheet on mobile) shows the running quote total and selected items, so users never lose context.
visual_reference: Think Shopify checkout flow meets equipment rental — each step is a focused task with clear progress indication. shadcn/ui Stepper pattern + Sheet component for the summary panel. Reference: Stitch mobile candidate (stitch-mobile-2) suggests card-based product selection which maps naturally to step 1.
strengths: Reduces cognitive load for first-time customers browsing 533+ products across 24 categories. Progressive disclosure prevents form abandonment — users see only what's relevant at each stage. The persistent summary creates a "shopping cart" mental model that event planners already understand. Works beautifully on mobile where screen real estate is limited.
tradeoffs: Slightly slower for repeat customers who know exactly what they want (mitigated by allowing direct-to-step navigation and a "reorder from previous quote" shortcut). More components to build than a single form. Step transitions need careful animation to feel fast, not bureaucratic.

OPTION_B:
label: "Single-page dynamic form with collapsible sections"
description: All quote fields on one scrollable page — equipment selection (accordion by category), date picker, delivery details, and contact info. Sections auto-expand as the user progresses. A floating "Submit Quote" CTA follows the viewport.
visual_reference: Linear-style single-page form with collapsible category accordions. shadcn/ui Accordion + Collapsible components. Sticky footer with quote total.
strengths: Faster for power users and returning customers — everything visible, no waiting for step transitions. Simpler state management (no multi-step routing). Users can jump to any section instantly. Fewer components to implement.
tradeoffs: Overwhelming for first-time visitors facing 24 equipment categories. Long scrollable forms have higher abandonment rates for complex selections. Harder to optimize for mobile — 533 products don't compress well into accordions on a 375px viewport. The "wall of form" impression can feel intimidating for a creative/events audience.

OPTION_C:
label: "Conversational quote builder (Morgan-integrated)"
description: Instead of a traditional form, the quote builder is a guided conversation with Morgan embedded inline. Users describe their event, Morgan suggests equipment, and the quote builds in a side panel. Falls back to manual selection if the user prefers.
visual_reference: Intercom-style embedded chat on the left, live quote document building on the right. Morgan chat widget (already in PRD) expanded to full-page context.
strengths: Perfectly aligned with the PRD's vision of "send Morgan a message, and it handles the rest." Differentiating experience that no competitor offers. Handles the complexity of 533 products by letting AI do the filtering. Natural upsell path (Morgan can suggest insurance, services, packages per the upsell skill).
tradeoffs: Highest implementation complexity — requires Morgan web chat to be fully functional at launch, which is a dependency risk. Users who prefer self-service may find it slower. Accessibility challenges with chat-based interfaces (screen readers, keyboard navigation). If Morgan's responses are slow (10-second SLA per PRD), the experience degrades. Not suitable as the sole path — needs a manual fallback, effectively doubling the work.

REASONING:
The multi-step wizard (Option A) is the right default for v1. Sigma-1's audience — event planners and production managers — are often browsing during early planning stages with incomplete requirements. A 533-product catalog across 24 categories is genuinely complex; progressive disclosure respects that complexity rather than dumping it all at once. The persistent summary sidebar directly supports the PRD's success criterion of "quote-to-invoice workflow completes in < 2 minutes" by keeping the user oriented throughout. Option C is compelling as a v2 enhancement once Morgan's web chat is battle-tested, but making it a launch dependency introduces too much risk. Option B is defensible for an admin/internal tool but wrong for a customer-facing conversion flow.
```

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-13
category: design-system
question: Should the frontend use shadcn/ui as-is, or extend it with custom components and tokens for Sigma-1 branding?
recommendation: B

OPTION_A:
label: "shadcn/ui vanilla with CSS variable overrides only"
description: Use shadcn/ui components exactly as generated, customizing only through Tailwind CSS variables (colors, border-radius, font family). No custom components beyond what shadcn/ui provides. Brand expression comes purely through color palette, typography choice, and imagery.
visual_reference: Default shadcn/ui aesthetic — clean, neutral, slightly clinical. Think linear.app or vercel.com dashboard. The zinc/slate default palette with Sigma-1's brand colors swapped into the primary/accent slots.
strengths: Fastest to ship. Zero design debt — every component is maintained upstream. New team members instantly productive (shadcn/ui docs are the docs). Consistent with the PRD's technology stack specification of "shadcn/ui" without qualification.
tradeoffs: The site will look like every other shadcn/ui site. For a lighting and visual production company, "clean and neutral" undersells the brand — Perception Events sells spectacle, drama, and atmosphere. No accommodation for industry-specific patterns (equipment cards with availability badges, quote line items, project timeline views). The current sigma-1.com has a bold, dark, high-contrast aesthetic that vanilla shadcn/ui won't capture.

OPTION_B:
label: "shadcn/ui extended with Sigma-1 design tokens and 5-8 custom components"
description: Start from shadcn/ui as the foundation, then layer: (1) A Sigma-1 token system — dark-first palette reflecting the lighting/production brand, custom type scale using a display font for headings, tighter spacing scale for data-dense views. (2) 5-8 custom components: EquipmentCard, AvailabilityBadge, QuoteLineItem, LeadScoreBadge, ProjectTimeline, ChatBubble, StatusPill, CategoryNav. These compose shadcn/ui primitives (Card, Badge, Table) but add domain-specific behavior and styling.
visual_reference: Dark mode primary (matching sigma-1.com's current dark aesthetic), with light mode as secondary. High-contrast accent colors (think stage lighting — amber, electric blue) against deep backgrounds. The Stitch mobile candidate (stitch-mobile-2) and Framer web candidate (framer-web-1) both suggest bold, visually rich layouts — this approach delivers that while staying in the shadcn/ui ecosystem.
strengths: Brand-differentiated without abandoning the shadcn/ui ecosystem. Domain-specific components accelerate feature development — an EquipmentCard with built-in availability state is reused across catalog, quote builder, and Morgan chat. The token system ensures consistency as the platform grows to include the Expo mobile app. Maintains upgradeability — custom components wrap shadcn/ui primitives, so upstream updates still flow through.
tradeoffs: 2-3 days additional setup time for the token system and initial custom components. Requires a lightweight style guide document so the team stays aligned. Custom components need maintenance — but with only 5-8, the surface area is manageable.

REASONING:
Sigma-1 is a lighting and visual production company. Their brand identity is literally about creating atmosphere and spectacle — vanilla shadcn/ui's clinical neutrality actively undermines that positioning. The current sigma-1.com already uses a dark, bold aesthetic which confirms this isn't hypothetical brand direction; it's established identity. Option B's 2-3 day investment in a token system and domain-specific components pays for itself immediately: an EquipmentCard component alone will be instantiated 533+ times across the catalog, quote builder, and search results. The key discipline is keeping the custom layer thin — 5-8 components that compose shadcn/ui primitives, not a parallel design system.
```

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-14
category: component-library
question: Which data table component should be used for equipment catalog and finance reporting in the frontend?
recommendation: B

OPTION_A:
label: "shadcn/ui Table + manual features"
description: Use shadcn/ui's Table component (which wraps a semantic HTML table) and hand-build sorting, filtering, and pagination using React state and URL search params. No additional dependencies.
visual_reference: shadcn/ui data table example (https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/data-table) — which itself recommends TanStack Table underneath. Clean, minimal rows with inline action menus.
strengths: Zero additional bundle size. Full control over every interaction. Simpler mental model for developers who just need basic display tables (e.g., invoice list with 5 columns).
tradeoffs: You'll end up rebuilding TanStack Table poorly. The equipment catalog needs faceted filtering across 24 categories, price ranges, availability dates, and specs — that's non-trivial state management. Finance reporting needs column sorting, row grouping (by status, by period), and export. With 533+ products, you'll eventually need virtualization for performance. Each of these features is a week of work to build correctly; TanStack Table ships them tested.

OPTION_B:
label: "TanStack Table v8 + shadcn/ui Table styling"
description: Use TanStack Table v8 as the headless logic layer (sorting, filtering, pagination, column visibility, row selection, virtualization) with shadcn/ui Table components for the visual layer. This is exactly the pattern shadcn/ui's own data-table example recommends. Add @tanstack/react-virtual for the 533-product catalog view.
visual_reference: shadcn/ui data-table example — TanStack Table powering the logic, shadcn/ui Table components rendering the cells. Faceted filter toolbar above the table for equipment categories, availability, and price range. Finance tables get column grouping and expandable row details.
strengths: Battle-tested logic for sorting, filtering, pagination, and virtualization — no reinvention. Headless architecture means styling stays 100% in shadcn/ui's design language (and the Sigma-1 token extensions from dp-13). shadcn/ui explicitly documents this integration path, so it's the blessed approach. Virtualization via @tanstack/react-virtual handles the 533-product catalog without DOM performance issues. Column visibility toggling lets users customize finance report views.
tradeoffs: ~15KB additional bundle (gzipped) for @tanstack/react-table + @tanstack/react-virtual. Learning curve for TanStack Table's column definition API — it's powerful but has an initial conceptual overhead. For very simple tables (3-4 rows, no interaction), TanStack Table is overkill — but you can still use plain shadcn/ui Table for those.

REASONING:
This is not a close call. The PRD specifies a 533-product equipment catalog with real-time availability filtering and finance reporting with AR aging, cash flow, and profitability views. These are data-dense, interactive surfaces that demand sorting, filtering, pagination, and (for the catalog) virtualization. shadcn/ui's own documentation explicitly recommends TanStack Table for data tables — choosing Option A means ignoring the library's own guidance and rebuilding what already exists. The 15KB bundle cost is trivial against the weeks of development time saved. Use plain shadcn/ui Table for simple display contexts (invoice detail line items, crew lists) and TanStack Table for the catalog and finance dashboards.
```

---

## Verification Checklist

- [x] All three design decision points (dp-12, dp-13, dp-14) addressed
- [x] Each option has label, description, strengths, and tradeoffs
- [x] A clear recommendation is stated for every decision (A, B, B)
- [x] Reasoning references PRD specifics: 533 products, 24 categories, quote-to-invoice workflow, sigma-1.com dark aesthetic, Morgan web chat, finance reporting requirements
- [x] No strawmen — Option A in each case is genuinely defensible (faster to ship, simpler to maintain)
- [x] Visual references included where provider candidates exist (Stitch mobile candidate, Framer web candidate, shadcn/ui documentation examples)
